overall_summary: Maven Solutions opposes Constellation's LCEP proposal, arguing it creates a tariff-enabled monopoly chokepoint at existing generation Points of Interconnection that enables incumbent gatekeeping, substitutes static thermal analysis for proven dynamic reliability, shifts costs onto captive ratepayers, and bypasses host communities entirely. If any expedited pathway is adopted, Maven urges eight structural conditions covering open access, full dynamic contingency studies, demonstrated protection design, full cost allocation to applicants, and mandatory community impact disclosure with environmental justice screening.

quotes.text: a tariff-enabled mechanism to convert incumbent control of existing generation Points of Interconnection into a gatekeeping product for the nation's fastest-growing source of electricity demand
quotes.concern: LCEP converts POI control into gatekeeping over large-load interconnection

quotes.text: the owner of an existing generation Point of Interconnection would control who gets expedited access to the transmission system for large-load interconnection
quotes.concern: incumbent generator gains veto power over expedited access

quotes.text: When Constellation asks FERC to create LCEP Service, it is asking the Commission to formalize the incumbent's gatekeeping power over a scarce, non-replicable asset—and to do so through a tariff mechanism that would apply across all Transmission Providers.
quotes.concern: LCEP formalizes bottleneck monopoly across all transmission providers

quotes.text: The consent requirement is not a procedural formality. It is the mechanism through which the incumbent monetizes its bottleneck position.
quotes.concern: generator consent functions as a rent-extraction tool

quotes.text: Unless FERC imposes structural safeguards—open, transparent, independently administered access rules with non-discrimination requirements—LCEP would constitute the kind of tariff-enabled gatekeeping that the Federal Power Act was designed to prevent.
quotes.concern: LCEP without structural safeguards violates FPA open-access principles

quotes.text: Constellation's Attachment A presents four scenarios showing that LCEP's net power flows stay within studied conditions. It omits the decisive fifth scenario: the paired load drops while new generation remains online.
quotes.concern: Constellation's reliability analysis omits the critical load-drop contingency

quotes.text: That footnote is doing the structural work of the entire reliability argument. It is not a protection design. It is not a clearing-time analysis. It is not a failure-mode study. It is a promise—and the Commission does not set just and reasonable rates on promises.
quotes.concern: Constellation's protection claim is an undemonstrated promise, not a design

quotes.text: Constellation's filing states that 'a load outage is not typically planned for currently and therefore should not need to be a part of the LCEP process studies.' That sentence was written one month after NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert—its highest-urgency notification—requiring registered entities to address computational load modeling, studies, instrumentation, commissioning, operations, protection, and control.
quotes.concern: Constellation dismisses load-outage planning after NERC's highest-priority alert required it

quotes.text: A megawatt netting table cannot substitute for validated dynamic models of computational loads with UPS systems, backup generation, power electronics, vendor-specific control logic, and autonomous protective behavior.
quotes.concern: static thermal tables are not a substitute for dynamic reliability modeling

quotes.text: The filing does not demonstrate who pays for the protection systems, remedial action schemes, telemetry, metering, dynamic fault recorders, communications, operator training, reserve requirements, voltage and reactive support, harmonics mitigation, restoration support, affected-system studies, network upgrades discovered through dynamic analysis, enforcement administration, or system impacts from protection misoperation.
quotes.concern: LCEP leaves a wide range of reliability and system costs unassigned

quotes.text: Every unaccounted cost that LCEP fails to assign to the applicant is a cost that falls on the ratepayer. The Commission should not adopt a replacement rate that treats cost allocation as an afterthought.
quotes.concern: unassigned LCEP costs shift onto captive ratepayers

quotes.text: A service that disappears when the host generator retires is not infrastructure. It is a monetization strategy for a depreciating asset—and the stranded-cost risk, when that asset retires, falls on the communities and ratepayers left behind.
quotes.concern: LCEP's dependency on the host generator exposes communities to stranded-cost risk at retirement

quotes.text: The word 'community' does not appear in the body of Constellation's filing. That is not an oversight. It is a design choice. LCEP is engineered to move fast by moving past the people who live nearest to the infrastructure.
quotes.concern: LCEP deliberately omits community considerations from its design

quotes.text: LCEP proposes to add industrial-scale data center load—with its own water demands, thermal discharge, noise, traffic, and fiscal dynamics—without any mechanism for those communities to know what is coming, negotiate terms, or hold anyone accountable.
quotes.concern: LCEP adds significant industrial burdens on host communities with no accountability mechanism

quotes.text: Maven Solutions' research documents 268 local opposition groups across 37 states, representing over 360,000 members, coordinated through organizations including the Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development.
quotes.concern: widespread community opposition signals that exclusion from siting decisions is a systemic failure

bins.name: LCEP Creates Bottleneck Monopoly
bins.description: Maven argues that LCEP converts incumbent control of existing generation Points of Interconnection into a gatekeeping product, giving large generators like Constellation veto power over who receives expedited large-load access. The consent requirement is not procedural but is the mechanism through which incumbents monetize a scarce, non-replicable asset. Without structural separation and non-discrimination requirements, this constitutes the kind of tariff-enabled gatekeeping the Federal Power Act was designed to prevent.

bins.name: Tariff Rules Must Be Transparent
bins.description: Maven calls for LCEP access rules to be open, transparent, and independently administered by the Transmission Provider, so that any entity meeting objective criteria can qualify on equal terms. The incumbent's consent cannot function as a veto or a toll.

bins.name: Dynamic Reliability Study Required
bins.description: Maven finds Constellation's reliability analysis fatally incomplete: it omits the scenario where load drops while generation stays online, producing an 800 MW export excursion above studied conditions. NERC's 2026 Level 3 Alert and Gap Assessment require computational-load dynamic modeling, yet Constellation's Attachment A offers only a static megawatt netting table. Maven argues a protection promise in a footnote cannot substitute for a full dynamic contingency study with validated models.

bins.name: Protection Design Must Be Demonstrated
bins.description: Maven contends that Constellation's reliability argument rests on a footnote assertion that 'mechanisms similar to those used in surplus or hybrid interconnection would be in place,' without a protection design basis, clearing-time analysis, or failure-mode study. The Commission cannot set a just and reasonable rate on an undemonstrated promise.

bins.name: Full Costs Must Flow to Applicants
bins.description: Maven argues LCEP's cost allocation is unanswered: Constellation's filing does not identify who pays for protection systems, remedial action schemes, studies, operator training, harmonics mitigation, or the many other costs that LCEP-enabled service would generate. Any unassigned cost falls on captive ratepayers who did not choose to host data centers. Maven also flags that LCEP's dependence on the host generator's continued operation exposes communities to stranded-cost risk at generator retirement.

bins.name: Co-location Favors Incumbents
bins.description: Maven argues that LCEP is structured as a co-location product that advantages existing generator owners: the incumbent's consent becomes the price of speed, giving it bargaining power in site deals, power purchase agreements, and tolling structures. Smaller generators and independent developers cannot replicate this advantage because they do not own the Points of Interconnection.

bins.name: Communities Excluded From LCEP Design
bins.description: Maven notes that the word 'community' does not appear in Constellation's filing, calling this a design choice rather than an oversight. LCEP would add industrial-scale data center load to existing power plant sites without disclosure, negotiation rights, or accountability mechanisms for host communities. Maven documents 268 local opposition groups across 37 states as evidence that exclusion from siting decisions is already a systemic failure, and argues a faster pathway that replicates this exclusion will deepen, not resolve, community opposition.
